lines.translation: Wang Peng and Li You study at the same school. They have known each other for almost half a year. Wang Peng often helps Li You practice speaking Chinese. They also often go out to have fun together, and they have a great time every time. Li You has a very good impression of Wang Peng, and Wang Peng likes Li You very much, too. They have become good friends.
The school is showing a Chinese movie this weekend. Shall we go see it together?
Great! But I heard that many people are going to see the movie. Can we get tickets?
I've already bought the tickets. It took a lot of effort to get them.
That's fantastic! I've wanted to see a Chinese movie for a long time. Is anyone else going with us?
No, just the two of us.
The day after tomorrow at eight in the evening.
Before the movie, I'll treat you to dinner.

vocabulary.definition: that settles it, that's settled, it's decided

example_sentence.py: tài hǎo le yī yán wéi dìng

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: This word cannot be used as a predicate. Even as an attributive, its usage is very limited.

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: Compare 李友对王朋的印象很好 (Lǐ Yǒu duì Wáng Péng de yìnxiàng hěn hǎo) (Li You has a very good impression of Wang Peng) and 李友给王朋的印象很好 (Lǐ Yǒu gěi Wáng Péng de yìnxiàng hěn hǎo) (Li You made a very good impression on Wang Peng).

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: The phrase 演电影 (yǎn diànyǐng) (to show a film) is interchangeable with 放电影 (fàng diànyǐng). In addition, 演电影 (yǎn diànyǐng) can also mean "to act in a film."

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: The primary meaning of 早 (zǎo) is “early," but in an extended sense it can also mean "a long time ago" or "early on."

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: This is a colloquial equivalent of 两个 (liǎng ge).

language_notes.label: f
language_notes.explanation_en: This phrase literally means "achieving certainty with one word" and is one of many four-character idioms that have their origins in classical Chinese and continue to be used by many native speakers of the language.

grammar_sections.title: Descriptive complements (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: The subject of a sentence can be described by a complement following 得 (de). The verbs (e.g., 玩, 笑), verb phrases (e.g., 打球), or adjectives (e.g., 高兴) give the causes, while the complements (e.g., 高兴, 可爱, 累, 又唱又跳) describe the effects on the subject. When an adjective serves as a descriptive complement, it is often preceded by the adverb 很 (hěn), just like a predicate adjective. A complement describing the subject seldom appears in the negative.

patterns.meaning_en: Used when a verb describes the cause and the complement describes the effect.
When the verb is a verb-object phrase, the verb must be repeated before 得.
Used when an adjective describes the cause and the complement describes the effect.

examples.translation: We had a happy time playing. (We played, and we were very happy.)
The kid gave a very cute smile. (The child smiled, and the child looked cute.)

examples.pinyin: Tā dǎ qiú dǎ de hěn lèi.
examples.translation: He was worn out from playing ball. (He played ball, and he was worn out.)

examples.pinyin: Tā gāoxìng de yòu chàng yòu tiào.
examples.translation: He was so happy that he ended up singing and dancing. (He was happy, and he was singing and dancing.)

usage_notes: The verb, verb phrase, or adjective before 得 indicates the cause, while the complement after 得 describes the effect on the subject.
When an adjective serves as a descriptive complement, it is often preceded by the adverb 很 (hěn), similar to a predicate adjective.
This type of complement seldom appears in the negative.

common_errors.wrong: 他高兴得没有又唱又跳。
common_errors.explanation_en: A complement describing the subject seldom appears in the negative.

grammar_sections.title: Potential complements (I)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 得 (de) or 不 (bu) is placed between a verb and a resultative or directional complement to indicate whether a certain result can be realized or not.

patterns.meaning_en: Affirmative form: indicates a certain result can be realized.
Negative form: indicates a certain result cannot be realized.

patterns.pattern: ...V + 得 + C + V + 不 + C?

examples.hanzi:
A: 你能学会跳舞吗?
B: 跳舞太难,我学不会。
examples.pinyin:
A: Nǐ néng xué huì tiào wǔ ma?
B: Tiàowǔ tài nán, wǒ xué bu huì.
examples.translation:
A: Can you learn to dance?
B: Dancing is too difficult. I can't learn it.

B: 我得开会,六点半回不来。

B: Wǒ děi kāi huì, liù diǎn bàn huí bu lái.
examples.translation:
A: Can you be back by 6:30 tonight?
B: I have to go to a meeting and won't make it back by 6:30.

examples.hanzi:
A: 这本书我考试要用,你今天看得完吗?
B: 这本书我今天看不完,你先用吧,我以后再看。
examples.pinyin:
A: Zhè běn shū wǒ kǎoshì yào yòng, nǐ jīntiān kàn de wán ma?
B: Zhè běn shū wǒ jīntiān kàn bu wán, nǐ xiān yòng ba, wǒ yǐhòu zài kàn.
examples.translation:
A: I need this book for an exam. Can you finish reading this book today?
B: I can't finish this book today. You use it first; I'll read it later.

A: 我看得懂。
examples.pinyin:

Q: 二十个饺子你吃得完吃不完?
A: 我吃得完。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Èrshí ge jiǎozi nǐ chī de wán chī bu wán?
A: Wǒ chī de wán.
examples.translation:

A: Yes, I can.

examples.translation: The door is too narrow. I can't go in.

usage_notes: Potential complements usually appear in negative sentences. They are used in affirmative sentences much less often.

In affirmative sentences, the '能 (néng) + verb + resultative/directional complement' structure is normally used. For example: 今天的功课不多,我很快就能做完。(There isn't much homework today. I can finish it very quickly.)
Potential complements can be used to ask questions. In these situations, we can answer affirmatively using potential complements.

Potential complements are often the only way to convey the idea that the absence of certain conditions prevents a result from being achieved. This function cannot be fulfilled by the '不能 (bù néng) + verb + complement' construction.

The meaning can change between a potential complement and a '不能' construction. For example, '我进不去' (wǒ jìn bu qù) implies inability due to a condition (the door is too narrow), while '你不能进去' (nǐ bù néng jìnqu) implies lack of permission (a meeting is in progress).

common_errors.wrong: 老师说得太快,我不能听清楚。
common_errors.correct: 老师说得太快,我听不清楚。
common_errors.explanation_en: If the inability to achieve a result is due to certain conditions, the potential complement (V + 不 + C) must be used, not '不能'.

common_errors.wrong: 我把今天的功课做不完。
common_errors.explanation_en: A potential complement cannot be used in a 把 (bǎ) sentence.

grammar_sections.title: 就 (jiù) (only, just)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: When used before a noun, pronoun, or verb, 就 (jiù) means "only" or "just." Often, the noun, pronoun, or verb can be modified by a numeral-measure word combination.

examples.hanzi: 我们班人很少,就七个学生。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bān rén hěn shǎo, jiù qī ge xuésheng.
examples.translation: Our class is small, with just seven students.

examples.hanzi: 今天功课很少,就写五个汉字。
examples.pinyin: Jīntiān gōngkè hěn shǎo, jiù xiě wǔ ge Hànzì.
examples.translation: There's little homework today. We only have to write five characters.

examples.hanzi: 我们一家五口,就我对味精过敏。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yì jiā wǔ kǒu, jiù wǒ duì wèijīng guòmǐn.
examples.translation: There are five people in our family. Only I am allergic to MSG.

examples.pinyin: Jīntiān wǒ jiù yǒu yì jié kè.
examples.translation: Today I have only one class.

language_practice_sections.title: A How did you feel?
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Ask and answer questions using descriptive complements to describe how an activity affected someone. Pattern: Verb + (Object) + Verb + 得 + Adj. + 不 + Adj.? Example Q: 你昨天晚上写汉字写得累不累？ (Nǐ zuótiān wǎnshang xiě Hànzì xiě de lèi bu lèi?) A: 我昨天晚上写汉字写得很累/不累。你呢？ (Wǒ zuótiān wǎnshang xiě Hànzì xiě de hěn lèi/bú lèi. Nǐ ne?)

drill_sentences.cn: 你每天上课上得累不累？

language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Potential Complement in A-not-A questions: Verb + de + Complement + Verb + bu + Complement?

language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Use 就 (jiù) to indicate exceptions in a question-answer format. (e.g., Q: 他们都...吗? A: 不, 就...)

character_definitions.name: 王朋
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A Chinese male college student, around 20 years old, with short black hair, wearing a casual hoodie and jeans. He has a warm, friendly, and slightly shy smile.

character_definitions.name: 李友
character_definitions.appearance_description_en: A female international student, around 20 years old, with long light brown hair, wearing a stylish but casual sweater and skirt. She looks curious, cheerful, and expressive.

They have become good friends.

global_config.teaching_style: A clear, encouraging, and structured approach that breaks down dialogue and concepts for foundational learning. The tone is friendly and supportive, focusing on building comprehension step-by-step.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern, and minimalist, using on-screen text and simple animations to highlight key information, suitable for educational cards.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the background of Wang Peng and Li You's friendship and learn key descriptive phrases.

narration.subtitle_en: This lesson begins with a narrator setting the scene. It tells us that Wang Peng and Li You study at the same school, using the word [zh:同] for 'same'. They've known each other for almost half a year. The narrator describes their interactions: Wang Peng helps Li You practice Chinese, and they often go out and have fun. The phrase [zh:玩儿得很高兴] means they have a great time playing. This structure describes the result of an action. Finally, it says Li You has a good impression of Wang Peng, and they have become good friends, using the verb [zh:成] for 'to become'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 王朋跟李友在同一个学校学习,他们认识已经快半年了。王朋常常帮李友练习说中文。他们也常常一起出去玩儿,每次都玩儿得很高兴。李友对王朋的印象很好,王朋也很喜欢李友,他们成了好朋友。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wáng péng gēn lǐ yǒu zài tóng yí ge xué xiào xué xí tā men rèn shi yǐ jīng kuài bàn nián le wáng péng cháng cháng bāng lǐ yǒu liàn xí shuō zhōng wén tā men yě cháng cháng yì qǐ chū qu wánr měi cì dōu wánr de hěn gāo xìng lǐ yǒu duì wáng péng de yìn xiàng hěn hǎo wáng péng yě hěn xǐ huan lǐ yǒu tā men chéng le hǎo péng you
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Wang Peng and Li You study at the same school. They have known each other for almost half a year. Wang Peng often helps Li You practice speaking Chinese. They also often go out to have fun together, and they have a great time every time. Li You has a very good impression of Wang Peng, and Wang Peng likes Li You very much, too. They have become good friends.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used before a noun with a measure word to mean 'the same one'.
Refers to the impression or image one has of someone or something.
A verb indicating a change of state or identity.
A descriptive complement explaining the result or state of the action 'to play'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the characters Wang Peng and Li You appearing at a school gate. Show icons for 'practicing Chinese' and 'having fun'.

segments.segment_title: Key Verbs: 印象 and 成
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use 'yìnxiàng' (impression) and 'chéng' (to become).

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on two important words from that sentence. First, [zh:印象]. This means 'impression'. The structure is 'A 对 B 的印象很好', meaning 'A has a good impression of B'. Notice the use of [zh:对], which means 'towards'. The second word is [zh:成], which means 'to become'. It's used to show a transformation. In the text, [zh:他们成了好朋友] means 'They became good friends'. It's a simple but powerful verb to show a change in relationship or status.

on_screen_text.notes: Key concepts for describing relationships and perceptions.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The structure 'A 对 B 的印象很好' means 'A has a good impression of B'.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 印 (yìn) means 'print' or 'stamp', and 象 (xiàng) means 'image'. Together, they form the idea of an image stamped in your mind.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates a change into a new state or role, often followed by a noun.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: The character itself depicts a weapon, but its meaning has evolved to signify completion or success, hence 'becoming' something.

segments.visual_notes: Show the structure 'A 对 B 的印象很好' with A and B replaced by character icons. Then show an arrow transforming two acquaintances into '好朋友' with the word '成' on the arrow.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to invite someone to an event and ask for their agreement.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, Wang Peng speaks. He starts with [zh:这个周末], 'this weekend'. He says the school is showing a Chinese movie. The verb here is [zh:演], which means 'to show' or 'to perform'. He then suggests, [zh:我们一起去看], 'let's go see it together'. The phrase [zh:一起] means 'together'. He ends with [zh:好吗], a common way to ask 'okay?' or 'how about it?' to seek agreement for a proposal. It makes the invitation polite and friendly.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这个周末学校演一个中国电影,我们一起去看,好吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhè ge zhōu mò xué xiào yǎn yí ge zhōng guó diàn yǐng wǒ men yì qǐ qù kàn hǎo ma
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The school is showing a Chinese movie this weekend. Shall we go see it together?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A time word indicating the end of the week.
In this context, it means a movie is being shown or screened.
A common word for film or motion picture.
An adverb indicating a joint action.

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar icon highlighting the weekend. Then, an animation of a movie screen with the word '演' on it.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the different meanings of 'yǎn diànyǐng' and its synonym.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the phrase [zh:演电影]. It's interesting because it has two common meanings. As used by Wang Peng, it means 'to show a film'. In this sense, it's interchangeable with another common phrase, [zh:放电影]. So, you could also say the school is going to [zh:放电影]. However, [zh:演电影] can also mean 'to act in a film'. The context usually makes the meaning clear. If a school or theater is the subject, it means 'to show'. If a person is the subject, it likely means 'to act'.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: To show a film / To act in a film

highlight_words.translation: to show, to perform, to act
highlight_words.explanation_en: A versatile verb related to performances and screenings.

highlight_words.translation: to show, to put on (a film)
highlight_words.explanation_en: A common synonym for '演' when referring to showing a movie.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons to differentiate the two meanings: a projector for 'to show' and an actor's mask for 'to act'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express agreement with a reservation and ask about possibility using a potential complement.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You agrees enthusiastically with [zh:好啊]! But then she adds a concern, starting with [zh:不过], which means 'however' or 'but'. She says, [zh:听说看电影的人很多], 'I heard many people are going to see the movie'. Her key question is [zh:买得到票吗]? This is a potential complement. [zh:买] is 'to buy', and [zh:到] indicates a successful result. The [zh:得] in the middle means the result is possible. So, she's asking, 'Can we get tickets?' or literally, 'Are tickets buy-able?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好啊！不过,听说看电影的人很多,买得到票吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo a búguò tīngshuō kàn diànyǐng de rén hěn duō mǎi de dào piào ma
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Great! But I heard that many people are going to see the movie. Can we get tickets?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to introduce a contrasting idea.

highlight_words.translation: to be told; to hear of
highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to introduce information that one has heard from others.
A potential complement indicating the possibility of successfully buying something.
A general term for tickets for movies, concerts, travel, etc.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a crowd of people around a ticket booth. Highlight the structure 'V + 得 + Result' in '买得到'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express that something took a lot of effort.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng reassures her. He says, [zh:票已经买好了], 'The tickets have already been bought'. Then he explains how he got them: [zh:我费了很大的力气才买到]. Let's break this down. [zh:费] means 'to spend'. [zh:力气] means 'strength' or 'effort'. So, [zh:费了很大的力气] means 'I spent a lot of effort'. The word [zh:才] emphasizes that the result was achieved only after this great effort. It implies difficulty. This is a very useful phrase to describe accomplishing something challenging.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 票已经买好了,我费了很大的力气才买到。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: piào yǐjīng mǎi hǎo le wǒ fèi le hěn dà de lìqi cái mǎi dào
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I've already bought the tickets. It took a lot of effort to get them.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action has already happened.
Used with abstract nouns like time or effort.
Often used with '费' to mean 'to expend effort'.
An adverb that emphasizes that something happened late or with difficulty.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of Wang Peng struggling to get tickets, then successfully holding them up. Highlight the phrase '费了很大的力气'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express excitement and ask if others are joining.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You is very happy. She exclaims [zh:好极了], which means 'Fantastic!' or 'Excellent!'. The structure 'adjective + [zh:极了]' is a common way to express an extreme degree. She then says, [zh:我早就想看中国电影了]. The key word here is [zh:早就], which means 'long ago' or 'for a long time'. It emphasizes that this is not a new desire. Finally, she asks, [zh:还有别人跟我们一起去吗]? [zh:别人] means 'other people'. So she's asking, 'Is anyone else going with us?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好极了！我早就想看中国电影了。还有别人跟我们一起去吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo jí le wǒ zǎojiù xiǎng kàn zhōngguó diànyǐng le hái yǒu biérén gēn wǒmen yìqǐ qù ma
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That's fantastic! I've wanted to see a Chinese movie for a long time. Is anyone else going with us?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used after an adjective to indicate the highest degree, like 'very' or 'extremely'.
Emphasizes that an action or state began much earlier than expected.
A pronoun referring to people other than oneself or the person being addressed.

segments.visual_notes: Animate sparkles around '好极了!'. Show a thought bubble from Li You with a movie screen, and a clock spinning backwards to indicate '早就'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a concise way to say 'just us two'.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng's reply is short and clear: [zh:没有,就我们俩]. The first part, [zh:没有], means 'No, there aren't'. The second part is very important. [zh:就] here means 'only' or 'just'. [zh:我们] means 'we' or 'us'. And [zh:俩] is a colloquial and very common way to say 'two people'. So, [zh:就我们俩] literally means 'just we two'. It's a very natural and efficient way to say 'It's just the two of us'.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: méi yǒu jiù wǒ men liǎ
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No, just the two of us.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used here to indicate that the scope is limited to what follows.
A numeral-measure word that is a contraction of '两个' (liǎng ge).

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a group of people, then cross it out with a red 'X' for '没有'. Then, show only the icons for Wang Peng and Li You, circled, with '就...俩' highlighted.

segments.teaching_goal: To master the use of 'jiù' for 'only' and 'liǎ' for 'two people'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on [zh:就] and [zh:俩]. The adverb [zh:就] is used before a noun or number to emphasize a small quantity, meaning 'only' or 'just'. For example, 'Our class is small, [zh:就七个学生], just seven students'. The word [zh:俩] is a handy shortcut. It means 'two people' and replaces the longer [zh:两个人]. You can say [zh:我们俩] for 'the two of us' or [zh:他们俩] for 'the two of them'. Remember, since [zh:俩] already includes the measure word, you don't need to add [zh:个] after it. So, [zh:我们俩] is correct, but '我们俩个' is not.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used before a noun or number to emphasize a small quantity.
A colloquial equivalent of '两个' (liǎng ge). Do not add '个' after it.

segments.visual_notes: Show an equation: '两 (liǎng) + 个 (ge) = 俩 (liǎ)'. Then show a red 'X' over '俩个'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard way to ask 'when'.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You's response is simple. First, [zh:好], 'Okay'. Then she asks for the time of the date: [zh:什么时候]? This is the most common way to ask 'when?' in Chinese. It's composed of [zh:什么], meaning 'what', and [zh:时候], meaning 'time' or 'moment'. Together, they literally mean 'what time?' or 'what moment?', which translates to 'when?'. It's a fundamental question phrase you will use all the time.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The standard question phrase for asking about time.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 什么 (shénme) 'what' + 时候 (shíhou) 'time/moment' = 'what time?' or 'when?'

segments.visual_notes: Animate a question mark morphing into a clock. Highlight the components '什么' and '时候'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state a specific future time.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng gives a precise time. He says [zh:后天晚上八点]. Let's break this down. [zh:后天] means 'the day after tomorrow'. [zh:晚上] means 'evening' or 'night'. And [zh:八点] means 'eight o'clock'. In Chinese, time expressions are ordered from largest unit to smallest. So, you have the day, then the part of the day, and finally the specific hour. This is a very clear and standard way to state a time.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hòu tiān wǎn shang bā diǎn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The day after tomorrow at eight in the evening.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A time word referring to two days from now.
Refers to the period after sunset.
The measure word for hours of the clock.

segments.visual_notes: Show a timeline: Today -> Tomorrow -> 后天. Then zoom in on '后天' and show a clock pointing to 8 with a moon icon for '晚上'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to invite someone for a meal and use the word 'yǐqián' (before).

narration.subtitle_en: Li You makes a counter-offer. She says, [zh:看电影以前,我请你吃晚饭]. The phrase [zh:看电影以前] means 'before the movie'. [zh:以前] means 'before' and it comes after the event it refers to. The main clause is [zh:我请你吃晚饭]. The verb [zh:请] means 'to treat' or 'to invite'. [zh:吃晚饭] means 'to eat dinner'. So, she is offering to treat him to dinner before they go to the movie. This is a very kind gesture.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 看电影以前,我请你吃晚饭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: kàn diànyǐng yǐqián wǒ qǐng nǐ chī wǎnfàn
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Before the movie, I'll treat you to dinner.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used after a verb or event to indicate a time prior to it.
Means to pay for someone's meal or entertainment as a host.
The evening meal.

segments.visual_notes: Show a timeline with two events: first, a dinner icon, then a movie icon. Draw an arrow from the movie back to the dinner, labeled '以前'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common idiom for sealing an agreement.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng is delighted. He says [zh:太好了], 'That's great!'. Then he uses a four-character idiom: [zh:一言为定]. This is a set phrase that means 'It's a deal!' or 'That settles it!'. Let's break it down: [zh:一] is 'one', [zh:言] is 'word', [zh:为] is 'becomes', and [zh:定] is 'settled' or 'fixed'. So, literally, 'one word becomes settled'. It's a firm and friendly way to confirm an arrangement, like a verbal handshake.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: tài hǎo le yī yán wéi dìng

highlight_words.translation: it's a deal; that settles it
highlight_words.explanation_en: A four-character idiom used to confirm an agreement firmly.

segments.visual_notes: Animate two hands shaking, with the characters '一言为定' appearing above them.

segments.segment_title: Idiom: 一言为定 (yì yán wéi dìng)
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the meaning and usage of the idiom 'yì yán wéi dìng'.

narration.subtitle_en: The phrase [zh:一言为定] is a great example of a 'chengyu', or Chinese idiom. These are often four-character phrases derived from classical stories or texts. While you don't need to know the origin story for every idiom, learning common ones like this will make your Chinese sound much more natural. You use [zh:一言为定] to enthusiastically confirm a plan or promise you've just made with someone. It shows you are serious and happy about the agreement. It's a perfect way to end a conversation about making plans.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It's a deal! / That settles it!

highlight_words.explanation_en: An idiom meaning 'one word and it's settled', used to finalize an agreement.

segments.visual_notes: Show a dialogue bubble with a plan, and a response bubble with '一言为定' and a checkmark icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use descriptive complements with '得' to describe the result or state of an action.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's dive into our first grammar point: descriptive complements. In the dialogue, we heard [zh:他们每次都玩儿得很高兴], 'They have a great time every time.' The word [zh:得] is used to connect a verb, which is the cause, to a complement that describes the effect. Here, the action is [zh:玩儿], to play, and the effect is [zh:很高兴], very happy. If the verb is a verb-object phrase, like [zh:打球] (to play ball), you must repeat the verb before [zh:得]. For example, [zh:他打球打得很累], 'He was worn out from playing ball.' This structure helps you describe the result or state of an action in detail.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: We had a happy time playing.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used after a verb or an adjective to connect it with a descriptive complement.
An adjective used here as a complement to describe the state resulting from the action '玩儿'.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Subject + Verb + de + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: The verb indicates the cause, and the complement describes the effect on the subject.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā dǎ qiú dǎ de hěn lèi.
grammar_points.explanation_en: When using a verb-object phrase, the verb must be repeated before '得'.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā gāoxìng de yòu chàng yòu tiào.
grammar_points.explanation_en: An adjective can also be the cause, describing the reason for the resulting action.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the sentence structure, highlighting the 'Verb + 得 + Complement' part. Use icons for 'play' and 'happy' to illustrate the cause and effect.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to form A-not-A questions using descriptive complements.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, how do you ask a question using this structure? You can use the A-not-A format with the adjective in the complement. For example, to ask if someone had a good time playing, you can say [zh:你上个周末玩儿得高兴不高兴?]. The pattern is 'Adjective-not-Adjective'. The answer would be either [zh:我玩儿得很高兴] or [zh:我玩儿得不高兴]. This is a very common way to form questions about the outcome of an activity.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你上个周末玩儿得高兴不高兴？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shàng ge zhōumò wánr de gāoxìng bu gāoxìng?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Did you have a good time playing last weekend?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The A-not-A form of the adjective '高兴' used to create a question.
Another example of the A-not-A question form for descriptive complements.

segments.visual_notes: Show the question and two possible answer paths (one for '高兴', one for '不高兴') branching out.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use potential complements with '得' and '不' to express whether an action can be realized.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at potential complements. These tell us whether an action can or cannot be completed due to certain conditions. In the dialogue, Li You asks, [zh:买得到票吗？], 'Can we get tickets?'. The structure is Verb + [zh:得] + Complement for the affirmative, meaning it's possible. For the negative, you use Verb + [zh:不] + Complement. For instance, [zh:我看不完] means 'I can't finish reading it,' perhaps because there's not enough time. This is different from saying [zh:我不能看完], which might imply a lack of permission. Potential complements are crucial for expressing ability or inability based on circumstances.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 这条中文短信你看得懂吗？ A: 我看得懂。/ 我看不懂。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Q: Zhè tiáo Zhōngwén duǎnxìn nǐ kàn de dǒng ma? A: Wǒ kàn de dǒng. / Wǒ kàn bu dǒng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Can you understand this Chinese text message? A: I can understand it. / I can't understand it.
on_screen_text.notes: Expresses possibility or impossibility based on conditions.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Affirmative potential complement: 'mǎi' (to buy) + 'de' + 'dào' (to arrive, to succeed).
Negative potential complement: 'kàn' (to read) + 'bu' + 'wán' (to finish).

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: V + de + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates that a certain result can be achieved.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: V + bu + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates that a certain result cannot be achieved due to conditions.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tiàowǔ tài nán, wǒ xué bu huì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Example: 'Dancing is too difficult, I can't learn it.'

segments.visual_notes: Use a split screen to show the affirmative 'V+得+C' with a checkmark and the negative 'V+不+C' with a cross.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to form A-not-A questions using potential complements.

narration.subtitle_en: To ask questions with potential complements, you can also use the A-not-A form. This time, the pattern is 'Verb-de-Complement Verb-bu-Complement'. For example, [zh:二十个饺子你吃得完吃不完?], 'Can you finish eating twenty dumplings or not?'. It's a direct way to ask about someone's capacity to do something. You can answer with the affirmative form, [zh:我吃得完], or the negative, [zh:我吃不完].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 二十个饺子你吃得完吃不完？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Èrshí ge jiǎozi nǐ chī de wán chī bu wán?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Can you finish eating twenty dumplings or not?

highlight_words.pinyin: chī de wán chī bu wán
highlight_words.explanation_en: The A-not-A question form for the potential complement '吃完'.

highlight_words.pinyin: zhǎo de dào zhǎo bu dào
highlight_words.explanation_en: Another example of the A-not-A question form for potential complements.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the question structure, highlighting the repeating verb and the '得/不' contrast.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '就' to mean 'only' or 'just', indicating a small or exclusive number.

narration.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is the adverb [zh:就]. When used before a noun or a number, it means 'only' or 'just,' emphasizing a small quantity. In the dialogue, Wang Peng says [zh:就我们俩], meaning 'Just the two of us.' This highlights that nobody else is going. Another example is [zh:我们班人很少，就七个学生], 'Our class is small, with just seven students.' Using [zh:就] is a simple way to stress that the number is small or exclusive.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Méi yǒu, jiù wǒ men liǎ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No, just the two of us.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used to emphasize that a number is small or that something is limited to a specific scope.
A colloquial term for '两个' (liǎng ge), meaning 'two people'.

grammar_points.pattern: 我们班人很少，就七个学生。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒmen bān rén hěn shǎo, jiù qī ge xuésheng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Emphasizes the small number of students.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Jīntiān wǒ jiù yǒu yì jié kè.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Emphasizes having only one class.

segments.visual_notes: Show a group of people, then animate a circle around just two of them as the word '就' appears.

segments.teaching_goal: To review and consolidate the key grammar points and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered descriptive complements to describe the result of an action, like [zh:玩儿得很高兴]. We also learned about potential complements to express ability, such as [zh:买得到票]. Finally, we saw how to use [zh:就] to mean 'only,' as in [zh:就我们俩]. Don't forget the useful idiom [zh:一言为定], 'It's a deal!'. Keep practicing these patterns!

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the background of Wang Peng and Li You's friendship and learn key descriptive phrases.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This lesson begins with a narrator setting the scene. It tells us that Wang Peng and Li You study at the same school, using the word [zh:同] for 'same'. They've known each other for almost half a year. The narrator describes their interactions: Wang Peng helps Li You practice Chinese, and they often go out and have fun. The phrase [zh:玩儿得很高兴] means they have a great time playing. This structure describes the result of an action. Finally, it says Li You has a good impression of Wang Peng, and they have become good friends, using the verb [zh:成] for 'to become'.

segments.segment_title: Key Verbs: 印象 and 成
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use 'yìnxiàng' (impression) and 'chéng' (to become).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on two important words from that sentence. First, [zh:印象]. This means 'impression'. The structure is 'A 对 B 的印象很好', meaning 'A has a good impression of B'. Notice the use of [zh:对], which means 'towards'. The second word is [zh:成], which means 'to become'. It's used to show a transformation. In the text, [zh:他们成了好朋友] means 'They became good friends'. It's a simple but powerful verb to show a change in relationship or status.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The structure 'A 对 B 的印象很好' means 'A has a good impression of B'.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 印 (yìn) means 'print' or 'stamp', and 象 (xiàng) means 'image'. Together, they form the idea of an image stamped in your mind.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates a change into a new state or role, often followed by a noun.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: The character itself depicts a weapon, but its meaning has evolved to signify completion or success, hence 'becoming' something.

content.notes: Key concepts for describing relationships and perceptions.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The structure 'A 对 B 的印象很好' means 'A has a good impression of B'.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 印 (yìn) means 'print' or 'stamp', and 象 (xiàng) means 'image'. Together, they form the idea of an image stamped in your mind.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates a change into a new state or role, often followed by a noun.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to invite someone to an event and ask for their agreement.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now, Wang Peng speaks. He starts with [zh:这个周末], 'this weekend'. He says the school is showing a Chinese movie. The verb here is [zh:演], which means 'to show' or 'to perform'. He then suggests, [zh:我们一起去看], 'let's go see it together'. The phrase [zh:一起] means 'together'. He ends with [zh:好吗], a common way to ask 'okay?' or 'how about it?' to seek agreement for a proposal. It makes the invitation polite and friendly.

content.focus_text: 这个周末学校演一个中国电影,我们一起去看,好吗？

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the phrase [zh:演电影]. It's interesting because it has two common meanings. As used by Wang Peng, it means 'to show a film'. In this sense, it's interchangeable with another common phrase, [zh:放电影]. So, you could also say the school is going to [zh:放电影]. However, [zh:演电影] can also mean 'to act in a film'. The context usually makes the meaning clear. If a school or theater is the subject, it means 'to show'. If a person is the subject, it likely means 'to act'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express agreement with a reservation and ask about possibility using a potential complement.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You agrees enthusiastically with [zh:好啊]! But then she adds a concern, starting with [zh:不过], which means 'however' or 'but'. She says, [zh:听说看电影的人很多], 'I heard many people are going to see the movie'. Her key question is [zh:买得到票吗]? This is a potential complement. [zh:买] is 'to buy', and [zh:到] indicates a successful result. The [zh:得] in the middle means the result is possible. So, she's asking, 'Can we get tickets?' or literally, 'Are tickets buy-able?'

content.focus_text: 好啊！不过,听说看电影的人很多,买得到票吗？

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express that something took a lot of effort.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng reassures her. He says, [zh:票已经买好了], 'The tickets have already been bought'. Then he explains how he got them: [zh:我费了很大的力气才买到]. Let's break this down. [zh:费] means 'to spend'. [zh:力气] means 'strength' or 'effort'. So, [zh:费了很大的力气] means 'I spent a lot of effort'. The word [zh:才] emphasizes that the result was achieved only after this great effort. It implies difficulty. This is a very useful phrase to describe accomplishing something challenging.

content.focus_text: 票已经买好了,我费了很大的力气才买到。

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You is very happy. She exclaims [zh:好极了], which means 'Fantastic!' or 'Excellent!'. The structure 'adjective + [zh:极了]' is a common way to express an extreme degree. She then says, [zh:我早就想看中国电影了]. The key word here is [zh:早就], which means 'long ago' or 'for a long time'. It emphasizes that this is not a new desire. Finally, she asks, [zh:还有别人跟我们一起去吗]? [zh:别人] means 'other people'. So she's asking, 'Is anyone else going with us?'

content.focus_text: 好极了！我早就想看中国电影了。还有别人跟我们一起去吗？
content.focus_pinyin: hǎo jí le wǒ zǎojiù xiǎng kàn zhōngguó diànyǐng le hái yǒu biérén gēn wǒmen yìqǐ qù ma
content.focus_gloss_en: That's fantastic! I've wanted to see a Chinese movie for a long time. Is anyone else going with us?

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a concise way to say 'just us two'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng's reply is short and clear: [zh:没有,就我们俩]. The first part, [zh:没有], means 'No, there aren't'. The second part is very important. [zh:就] here means 'only' or 'just'. [zh:我们] means 'we' or 'us'. And [zh:俩] is a colloquial and very common way to say 'two people'. So, [zh:就我们俩] literally means 'just we two'. It's a very natural and efficient way to say 'It's just the two of us'.

content.focus_pinyin: méi yǒu jiù wǒ men liǎ
content.focus_gloss_en: No, just the two of us.

segments.teaching_goal: To master the use of 'jiù' for 'only' and 'liǎ' for 'two people'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on [zh:就] and [zh:俩]. The adverb [zh:就] is used before a noun or number to emphasize a small quantity, meaning 'only' or 'just'. For example, 'Our class is small, [zh:就七个学生], just seven students'. The word [zh:俩] is a handy shortcut. It means 'two people' and replaces the longer [zh:两个人]. You can say [zh:我们俩] for 'the two of us' or [zh:他们俩] for 'the two of them'. Remember, since [zh:俩] already includes the measure word, you don't need to add [zh:个] after it. So, [zh:我们俩] is correct, but '我们俩个' is not.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used before a noun or number to emphasize a small quantity.
A colloquial equivalent of '两个' (liǎng ge). Do not add '个' after it.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard way to ask 'when'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You's response is simple. First, [zh:好], 'Okay'. Then she asks for the time of the date: [zh:什么时候]? This is the most common way to ask 'when?' in Chinese. It's composed of [zh:什么], meaning 'what', and [zh:时候], meaning 'time' or 'moment'. Together, they literally mean 'what time?' or 'what moment?', which translates to 'when?'. It's a fundamental question phrase you will use all the time.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The standard question phrase for asking about time.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 什么 (shénme) 'what' + 时候 (shíhou) 'time/moment' = 'what time?' or 'when?'

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state a specific future time.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng gives a precise time. He says [zh:后天晚上八点]. Let's break this down. [zh:后天] means 'the day after tomorrow'. [zh:晚上] means 'evening' or 'night'. And [zh:八点] means 'eight o'clock'. In Chinese, time expressions are ordered from largest unit to smallest. So, you have the day, then the part of the day, and finally the specific hour. This is a very clear and standard way to state a time.

content.focus_pinyin: hòu tiān wǎn shang bā diǎn
content.focus_gloss_en: The day after tomorrow at eight in the evening.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to invite someone for a meal and use the word 'yǐqián' (before).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You makes a counter-offer. She says, [zh:看电影以前,我请你吃晚饭]. The phrase [zh:看电影以前] means 'before the movie'. [zh:以前] means 'before' and it comes after the event it refers to. The main clause is [zh:我请你吃晚饭]. The verb [zh:请] means 'to treat' or 'to invite'. [zh:吃晚饭] means 'to eat dinner'. So, she is offering to treat him to dinner before they go to the movie. This is a very kind gesture.

content.focus_text: 看电影以前,我请你吃晚饭。

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a common idiom for sealing an agreement.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng is delighted. He says [zh:太好了], 'That's great!'. Then he uses a four-character idiom: [zh:一言为定]. This is a set phrase that means 'It's a deal!' or 'That settles it!'. Let's break it down: [zh:一] is 'one', [zh:言] is 'word', [zh:为] is 'becomes', and [zh:定] is 'settled' or 'fixed'. So, literally, 'one word becomes settled'. It's a firm and friendly way to confirm an arrangement, like a verbal handshake.

content.focus_pinyin: tài hǎo le yī yán wéi dìng

segments.segment_title: Idiom: 一言为定 (yì yán wéi dìng)
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the meaning and usage of the idiom 'yì yán wéi dìng'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The phrase [zh:一言为定] is a great example of a 'chengyu', or Chinese idiom. These are often four-character phrases derived from classical stories or texts. While you don't need to know the origin story for every idiom, learning common ones like this will make your Chinese sound much more natural. You use [zh:一言为定] to enthusiastically confirm a plan or promise you've just made with someone. It shows you are serious and happy about the agreement. It's a perfect way to end a conversation about making plans.

content.focus_gloss_en: It's a deal! / That settles it!

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use descriptive complements with '得' to describe the result or state of an action.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's dive into our first grammar point: descriptive complements. In the dialogue, we heard [zh:他们每次都玩儿得很高兴], 'They have a great time every time.' The word [zh:得] is used to connect a verb, which is the cause, to a complement that describes the effect. Here, the action is [zh:玩儿], to play, and the effect is [zh:很高兴], very happy. If the verb is a verb-object phrase, like [zh:打球] (to play ball), you must repeat the verb before [zh:得]. For example, [zh:他打球打得很累], 'He was worn out from playing ball.' This structure helps you describe the result or state of an action in detail.

content.focus_gloss_en: We had a happy time playing.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Subject + Verb + de + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: The verb indicates the cause, and the complement describes the effect on the subject.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā dǎ qiú dǎ de hěn lèi.
grammar_points.explanation_en: When using a verb-object phrase, the verb must be repeated before '得'.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā gāoxìng de yòu chàng yòu tiào.
grammar_points.explanation_en: An adjective can also be the cause, describing the reason for the resulting action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used after a verb or an adjective to connect it with a descriptive complement.
An adjective used here as a complement to describe the state resulting from the action '玩儿'.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Subject + Verb + de + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: The verb indicates the cause, and the complement describes the effect on the subject.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā dǎ qiú dǎ de hěn lèi.
grammar_points.explanation_en: When using a verb-object phrase, the verb must be repeated before '得'.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā gāoxìng de yòu chàng yòu tiào.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to form A-not-A questions using descriptive complements.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now, how do you ask a question using this structure? You can use the A-not-A format with the adjective in the complement. For example, to ask if someone had a good time playing, you can say [zh:你上个周末玩儿得高兴不高兴?]. The pattern is 'Adjective-not-Adjective'. The answer would be either [zh:我玩儿得很高兴] or [zh:我玩儿得不高兴]. This is a very common way to form questions about the outcome of an activity.

content.focus_text: 你上个周末玩儿得高兴不高兴？
content.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shàng ge zhōumò wánr de gāoxìng bu gāoxìng?
content.focus_gloss_en: Did you have a good time playing last weekend?

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use potential complements with '得' and '不' to express whether an action can be realized.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at potential complements. These tell us whether an action can or cannot be completed due to certain conditions. In the dialogue, Li You asks, [zh:买得到票吗？], 'Can we get tickets?'. The structure is Verb + [zh:得] + Complement for the affirmative, meaning it's possible. For the negative, you use Verb + [zh:不] + Complement. For instance, [zh:我看不完] means 'I can't finish reading it,' perhaps because there's not enough time. This is different from saying [zh:我不能看完], which might imply a lack of permission. Potential complements are crucial for expressing ability or inability based on circumstances.

content.focus_text: Q: 这条中文短信你看得懂吗？ A: 我看得懂。/ 我看不懂。
content.focus_pinyin: Q: Zhè tiáo Zhōngwén duǎnxìn nǐ kàn de dǒng ma? A: Wǒ kàn de dǒng. / Wǒ kàn bu dǒng.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: Can you understand this Chinese text message? A: I can understand it. / I can't understand it.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: V + bu + Complement
grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates that a certain result cannot be achieved due to conditions.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tiàowǔ tài nán, wǒ xué bu huì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Example: 'Dancing is too difficult, I can't learn it.'

content.notes: Expresses possibility or impossibility based on conditions.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Affirmative potential complement: 'mǎi' (to buy) + 'de' + 'dào' (to arrive, to succeed).
Negative potential complement: 'kàn' (to read) + 'bu' + 'wán' (to finish).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to form A-not-A questions using potential complements.

narration_track.subtitle_en: To ask questions with potential complements, you can also use the A-not-A form. This time, the pattern is 'Verb-de-Complement Verb-bu-Complement'. For example, [zh:二十个饺子你吃得完吃不完?], 'Can you finish eating twenty dumplings or not?'. It's a direct way to ask about someone's capacity to do something. You can answer with the affirmative form, [zh:我吃得完], or the negative, [zh:我吃不完].

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '就' to mean 'only' or 'just', indicating a small or exclusive number.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is the adverb [zh:就]. When used before a noun or a number, it means 'only' or 'just,' emphasizing a small quantity. In the dialogue, Wang Peng says [zh:就我们俩], meaning 'Just the two of us.' This highlights that nobody else is going. Another example is [zh:我们班人很少，就七个学生], 'Our class is small, with just seven students.' Using [zh:就] is a simple way to stress that the number is small or exclusive.

segments.teaching_goal: To review and consolidate the key grammar points and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered descriptive complements to describe the result of an action, like [zh:玩儿得很高兴]. We also learned about potential complements to express ability, such as [zh:买得到票]. Finally, we saw how to use [zh:就] to mean 'only,' as in [zh:就我们俩]. Don't forget the useful idiom [zh:一言为定], 'It's a deal!'. Keep practicing these patterns!
